title: Tariffs, Turbulence, and Trump: The Economist's Discourse on the US Trade War
author: Kalina Ishpekova-Bratanova (University of National and World Economy, Bulgaria)
body:
This paper applies Critical Discourse Analysis (CDA) to The Economist's commentaries published between February and March 2025, analyzing how the publication discursively constructs the US tariff war. The analysis identifies several key thematic dimensions including erratic decision-making characterized by shifting tariff announcements and reversals, rejection of established multilateral trade norms in favor of bilateral coercion, and the instrumentalization of tariffs for non-trade objectives such as immigration control and drug enforcement.

The study finds that The Economist frames the ongoing trade war as a fundamentally politically-driven departure from previous norms of stable, rules-based, and cooperative trade policy. The consequences highlighted include heightened market volatility, supply chain disruption, persistent inflation, weakened investor confidence across major economies, and a long-term erosion of trust in global trade institutions. The discursive construction positions these developments not as tactical adjustments within an established paradigm, but as a structural transformation of the global trading order with far-reaching implications for international economic governance.

title: Financial Inclusion, Infrastructure Development, Renewable Energy and Environmental Degradation
author: Multi-national Research Team
body:
The relationship between economic development and environmental quality has been a central concern in both academic and policy circles for decades. The Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC) hypothesis posits an inverted U-shaped relationship between per capita income and environmental degradation: as an economy develops, environmental degradation initially increases, reaches a peak, and then declines as income continues to rise. However, empirical evidence for this hypothesis remains mixed and highly context-dependent.

This study investigates the role of financial inclusion as a mediating factor in the relationship between infrastructure development, renewable energy adoption, and carbon emissions across 30 OECD countries from 2000 to 2020. Using panel data analysis with fixed effects and generalized method of moments (GMM) estimators, we find that higher levels of financial inclusion are associated with accelerated renewable energy adoption, but only when accompanied by robust regulatory frameworks. Infrastructure development exhibits a curvilinear correlation with carbon emissions, supporting the EKC hypothesis in the context of developed economies.

Our findings suggest that policies promoting financial inclusion must be coordinated with environmental regulation and renewable energy incentives to achieve sustainable development outcomes. The implications for developing economies, where financial inclusion rates remain below 50%, are particularly significant.

title: Beyond the Inflection Point: The New Forces Shaping the Transformation of Work
author: Himanshu Palsule (CEO, Cornerstone OnDemand) & Till Leopold (WEF)
body:
We have passed a critical inflection point. The convergence of three powerful forces — demographic shifts, deglobalization, and the acceleration of artificial intelligence — is exposing systemic weaknesses in the global labour market. Employers anticipate that 39% of core skills will change by 2030, according to the World Economic Forum's Future of Jobs 2025 report. There are currently 1.4 million unfilled technology positions globally, while a projected shortfall of 11 million healthcare workers looms by the end of the decade.

Perhaps most significantly, artificial intelligence is fundamentally redefining what constitutes 'skilled labour.' The traditional separation between 'technical jobs' and 'people jobs' is collapsing. Increasingly, roles require a 50-50 blend of technical and human capabilities — the programmer who must also communicate with stakeholders, the nurse who must interpret AI-generated diagnostic data, the teacher who must curate personalized digital learning pathways.

The disruption is particularly acute at the entry level. Organizations estimate they could automate 30% of entry-level work hours using current AI technologies. Computer science graduates now face a 129% higher unemployment rate for junior developer positions compared to pre-AI benchmarks, even as 1.6 million AI specialist positions remain unfilled globally. This paradox — simultaneous labour shortages and surpluses — signals a fundamental breakdown in the pipeline connecting education to employment.

title: The Guardian View on Development's Paradox: The Rich Benefit More Than the Poor
author: The Guardian Editorial Board
body:
A 2022 study found that from 1990 to 2015, wealthy nations 'drained' the developing world of approximately $242 trillion (in 2010 prices) — roughly a quarter of global-north income — through mechanisms of unequal exchange that operate without the overt coercion of colonialism. The World Bank subsequently reported that the rich world earned over $1.4 trillion in loan repayments from developing nations in 2023 alone, with projections exceeding $2 trillion annually by 2030.

The structure of this inequality is deeply embedded in the architecture of global finance and trade. Poor nations borrow in rich-world currencies to pay for essential imports — energy, food, medicine — while exporting predominantly lower-value commodities and manufactured goods. The terms of trade are structurally asymmetric: developing countries face higher borrowing costs, restricted market access for their manufactured exports, and limited policy space to pursue industrial upgrading strategies.

Cambridge economist Jostein Hauge, in his recent work The Future of the Factory, argues that industrial production remains vital for development trajectories, yet rich nations systematically block fair market access for global south manufacturers while simultaneously avoiding meaningful ecological responsibility. The climate dimension adds further injustice: low-income countries account for a mere 1% of global excess resource use, yet are disproportionately pressured into green transition policies with negligible financial and technological support from the industrialized world.

title: Jobs of Tomorrow: Technology and the Future of the World's Largest Workforces 2025
author: World Economic Forum
body:
Agriculture (approximately 25% of global employment): AI-driven predictive analytics, autonomous tractors, robotic harvesters, and drone-based crop monitoring are transforming traditional farming. However, smallholder farmers in lower-income countries face significant barriers to technology adoption, including limited access to capital, digital infrastructure gaps, and insufficient technical training. The risk is a widening productivity gap between large-scale industrial agriculture in developed economies and smallholder farming in the developing world.

Manufacturing (approximately 14% of global employment): The integration of AI-enabled visual quality control systems, autonomous mobile robots, and generative AI-guided robotic manipulators is reshaping production floors. The impact on employment levels depends critically on the mode of adoption — whether technologies complement or substitute human workers — and on national investment capacity for workforce retraining.

Construction (approximately 8% of global employment): Semi-automated bricklaying systems, large-scale 3D printing and prefabrication, computer vision for real-time hazard detection, and fully robotic dam construction (exemplified by the Kawakami dam project in Japan) are progressively shifting human roles from manual execution toward planning, monitoring, and quality assurance.

Wholesale & Retail Trade (approximately 13% of global employment): E-commerce automation, AI-driven inventory management, and autonomous last-mile delivery systems are fundamentally restructuring the sector, with disproportionate impacts on entry-level and low-skilled positions.
